← World Gallery
Regions Artists Timeline All works

Africa

Bronze, stone, cloth, and mud — craft from across the continent.

Africa's art tradition is vast and old. These pieces range from 9th-century Igbo bronzes to the largest mud-brick building in the world.

Click any image to enlarge. Use ← → or close with Esc.

Before 1000 BCE

Blombos Cave Engravings
Middle Stone Age · c. 75,000 BCE · South Africa
Deliberate crosshatch patterns incised on ochre and shell beads perforated with bone awls — among the earliest evidence of symbolic thought ever found. The cave pushed back the dawn of human art by tens of thousands of years.
Tsodilo Hills Rock Paintings
San · c. 24,000 BCE–1800 CE · Botswana
More than four thousand paintings on the quartzite outcrops the San call 'the Louvre of the desert.' Eland and giraffes predominate, but red-pigment handprints dating to the last Ice Age make this one of the longest continuously used sacred sites on earth.
Laas Geel Rock Paintings
Neolithic pastoralist · c. 3500 BCE · Somaliland
A complex of granite shelters filled with some of Africa's best-preserved rock art: cattle draped in ceremonial robes, their horns painted in alternating red and white, with small human figures raising arms beneath them. Only rediscovered by archaeologists in 2002.

1000 BCE – 0

Yeha Great Temple
D'mt kingdom · c. 700 BCE · Ethiopia
A rectangular limestone sanctuary built without mortar, oriented to the rising of the moon god Almaqah. Its precise dry-stone masonry is the oldest standing building in Ethiopia and predates the Aksumite kings by nearly a millennium.
Nok Terracotta
Nok culture · 500 BCE – 500 CE · Nigeria
Hollow terracotta heads with triangular eyes and pierced pupils. The oldest known figurative sculpture in sub-Saharan Africa.

0 – 500 CE

San Rock Art
San · up to 30,000 years old · Southern Africa
Eland, hunters, and trance figures painted on rock shelters from Namibia to the Drakensberg. One of humanity's longest unbroken painting traditions.
Brandberg Rock Paintings
San · up to 2,000 years old · Namibia
The Brandberg Massif hides thousands of rock paintings, most famously the "White Lady" — not a lady at all, but a medicine figure. Painted for ritual, not ornament.
Twyfelfontein Petroglyphs
Pre-San hunter-gatherers · 6,000 years old · Namibia
Thousands of rock engravings of giraffes, rhinos, and lions on sandstone. A seal, 900 km from the nearest sea, suggests long-distance travel — or long memory.
Obelisks of Aksum
Aksumite kingdom · 4th c. · Ethiopia
Granite stelae marking royal graves; the tallest once stood nearly a hundred feet high. One was looted by Mussolini, returned in 2005.

500 – 1000

Lydenburg Heads
Early Iron Age · 500 CE · South Africa
Terracotta heads found buried under a farm in Mpumalanga — the earliest known African figurative sculpture south of the equator.
Debre Damo Monastery
Aksumite · 6th century · Ethiopia
Perched atop a flat-topped mountain reachable only by a 15-meter rope hauled up by the monks. Its interior preserves carved wooden coffers and rafters that are the oldest surviving examples of Aksumite architecture.
Igbo-Ukwu Bronze
Igbo · 9th c. · Nigeria
Lost-wax castings of startling delicacy — three centuries before Ife, and predating most European bronzework of the same technical sophistication.

1000 – 1500

Mapungubwe Hill
Mapungubwe Kingdom · 1075–1220 · South Africa
A sandstone hill-top capital that controlled Indian Ocean gold and ivory trade centuries before Great Zimbabwe. A golden rhinoceros was dug up from a royal grave here.
Ethiopian Processional Cross
Ethiopian Orthodox · 12th c. onward · Ethiopia
Hand-held metal crosses carried at the front of church processions. Each is unique — geometries nested inside geometries, hammered out by a parish smith.
Cairo Citadel
Ayyubid → Ottoman · 1176 onward · Egypt
Saladin's fortress above old Cairo, topped centuries later by the Mosque of Muhammad Ali with its tall twin minarets — a silhouette that defines the skyline.
Great Mosque of Kilwa
Swahili coast · 11th–14th c. · Tanzania
Coral-stone mosque on an island that dominated Indian Ocean trade. Ibn Battuta called Kilwa "one of the most beautiful cities in the world."
Rock-Hewn Churches of Lalibela
Zagwe dynasty · 12th–13th c. · Ethiopia
Eleven churches carved downward into solid rock. Meant as a new Jerusalem after pilgrimage routes were cut off.
Bandiagara Escarpment
Tellem and Dogon · c. 1000 CE onward · Mali
A 150-kilometer sandstone cliff that the Tellem used as burial shafts before the Dogon arrived in the fifteenth century. Granaries and shrines cling to the rockface like swallows' nests; the Dogon say their cosmology is written into the architecture.
Church of Saint George, Lalibela
Zagwe dynasty · late 12th–early 13th century · Ethiopia
Carved top-down into a single block of red volcanic tuff in the shape of a Greek cross, thirty meters on a side. King Lalibela commissioned eleven such monolithic churches after Jerusalem fell to Saladin, intending to build a new Holy Land in the Ethiopian highlands.
Great Zimbabwe
Shona · 11th–15th c. · Zimbabwe
Dry-stone walls built without mortar, enclosing a trade capital that controlled the gold road to the Indian Ocean.
Great Mosque of Djenné
Mali · rebuilt 1907 (earlier mosque 13th c.)
The world's largest mud-brick building. Every spring the whole town replasters it by hand in a single day.
Golden Rhinoceros of Mapungubwe
Mapungubwe · c. 1220–1290 CE · South Africa
A small wooden figurine plated with hammered gold foil, unearthed from a royal grave on Mapungubwe Hill in 1934. Apartheid-era archaeology hid the find for decades because it proved sophisticated African statehood before European contact.
Bronze Head from Ife
Yoruba · 12th–15th c. · Nigeria
Naturalistic portraits cast in brass with lost-wax technique. When first seen in Europe they were disbelieved as African work.
Sankore Madrasa
Mali/Songhai · c. 989 onward · Timbuktu
An earthen mosque-university whose pyramidal minaret's dimensions match the Kaaba in Mecca. Its scholars curated one of the largest libraries in medieval Africa; tens of thousands of manuscripts from Sankore survive, hidden in family chests when Tuareg raiders closed in.
Djinguereber Mosque
Mali Empire · 1327 · Timbuktu
Commissioned by Mansa Musa after his pilgrimage to Mecca. An Andalusian architect built it in earth and fossil limestone — one of three medieval mosques still standing in Timbuktu.
Dogon Cliff Villages
Dogon · 14th c. onward · Mali
Mud granaries and shrines stacked along the Bandiagara Escarpment. The Dogon moved here fleeing forced conversion and stayed half a millennium.
Timbuktu Manuscripts
West African Islamic · 13th c. onward · Mali
Hundreds of thousands of hand-copied manuscripts on astronomy, law, medicine, poetry. Families smuggled them out of Timbuktu in 2013 as jihadists approached, hiding them in cellars and metal chests.
Larabanga Mosque
Sudano-Sahelian · 15th c. · Ghana
A mud-and-stick mosque painted white, bristling with wooden supports. The oldest mosque in West Africa still in its original form.
Ethiopian Illuminated Bible
Ethiopian Orthodox · 15th c. onward
Ge'ez-language bibles painted on parchment with bold figural interior pages — a distinctly Ethiopian Christian visual tradition that ran continuously from late antiquity to the 20th century.
Elmina Castle
Portuguese colonial · 1482 · Ghana
Whitewashed on a rock above the Gulf of Guinea — the oldest European building in sub-Saharan Africa. Started as a trading post for gold, became a holding point for the Atlantic slave trade.
Tomb of Askia
Songhai Empire · 1495 · Gao, Mali
A seventeen-meter pyramidal mud tower bristling with protruding palm-wood timbers, built for emperor Askia Mohammed after his pilgrimage to Mecca. The timbers double as permanent scaffolding for the annual replastering of its earthen skin.

1500 – 1800

Great Mosque of Kano
Hausa · 15th c. onward · Nigeria
A mud-and-stick Friday mosque rebuilt many times across six centuries. The current building rose after a 1937 fire; minarets slender as reeds above a walled city.
Swahili Stone Towns
Swahili coast · 12th–19th c. · East Africa
Coral-stone trading towns — Lamu, Pate, Kilwa — with carved wooden doors studded in brass, Indian Ocean dhows in their harbors, and mosques whose minarets face inland.
Khami Ruins
Torwa state · c. 1450–1650 · Zimbabwe
A stone city of terraced platforms retained by decorative chevron and checker patterns in dry-laid granite. Successor to Great Zimbabwe, Khami traded Ming porcelain and Portuguese beads with the east African coast before being burned in the seventeenth century.
Bronze Head of Queen Idia
Benin kingdom · 16th c. · Nigeria
Portrait of the first Queen Mother of Benin, cast to sit on an ancestral altar. One of the most famous of the Benin bronzes.
Queen Mother Pendant Mask (Iyoba)
Edo kingdom · 16th c. · Nigeria
Ivory mask worn on the belt of the Oba of Benin. The forehead tiaras are miniature Portuguese heads — trading partners carved as ornament.
Harar Jugol
Walled old city · 16th century · Ethiopia
Called Islam's fourth holy city, with 82 mosques and 102 shrines packed inside its five-gated wall. Harari houses are painted bright interior hues and display hung baskets — the craft tradition of married women signaling wealth and taste.
Benin Bronzes
Edo kingdom · 16th–17th c. · Nigeria
Plaques that once covered pillars of the royal palace in Benin City. Looted in 1897; still being repatriated today.
Fasil Ghebbi
Ethiopian · 17th c. · Gondar
A fortress-city of stone castles built by a succession of Solomonic emperors. The European-looking keeps are misleading — they're Ethiopian invention, not import.
Kente Cloth
Asante & Ewe · 17th c. onward · Ghana
Hand-woven silk and cotton strips sewn together into royal cloth. Each pattern has a name and a proverb behind it.
Akan Goldweights
Asante / Akan · 15th–19th c. · Ghana
Brass figurines used to weigh gold dust. Each one is also a proverb — the weight you balance against is a riddle.
Ndop — Kuba King Portrait
Kuba Kingdom · 17th c. onward · DR Congo
Wooden royal portraits showing each Kuba king with a personal emblem — a drum, an anvil, a game board. Stand-ins who keep the king present after death.
Baule Sculpture
Baule · 17th c. onward · Côte d'Ivoire
Delicate wooden figures of "spirit spouses" from a parallel world, and calm portrait masks carved for Goli festivals. Among the most influential African sculptural traditions.
Cape Coast Castle
Colonial fortification · 1653 onward · Ghana
A whitewashed castle on the Gulf of Guinea used for the Atlantic slave trade — enslaved Africans passed through its "Door of No Return." Now a museum, with whitewash still yearly.
Koutammakou (Takienta Tower-Houses)
Batammariba · 17th century onward · Togo/Benin
Fortified two-story mud towers with conical thatched granary turrets, built by the Batammariba on the Togo-Benin border. Each house is read as a human body — entrance mouth, granary head — and its construction ritualized every stage of a family's life.
Golden Stool of the Asante
Asante · 18th c. · Ghana
Sacred seat that is said to contain the soul of the Asante nation. No one — not even the king — sits on it. A British demand to do so in 1900 sparked war.
Adinkra Symbols
Akan / Asante · 18th c. onward · Ghana
A system of visual proverbs stamped or woven into cloth — each glyph carrying a name and an aphorism. Worn at funerals; later embraced globally as a Pan-African vocabulary.

1800 onward

Kuba Appliqué Textiles
Kuba Kingdom · 17th c. onward · DR Congo
Raffia cloth embroidered with cut-pile geometrics. Men weave the base; women embroider. No two patterns repeat — each is a one-time composition.
Senufo Sculpture
Senufo · West Africa
Elongated female figures and carved divination stools used by the Poro and Sandogo societies in Côte d'Ivoire and Mali.
Ibeji Twin Figures
Yoruba · 19th c. onward · Nigeria
When a twin dies, a carved figure stands in — fed, dressed, and cared for like the living. The Yoruba have one of the highest twinning rates in the world.
Asante Jewelry
Akan / Asante · 19th c. · Ghana
Gold jewelry for the chief and his court — beads, pectorals, rings cast with the same lost-wax technique as the goldweights. Sumptuary law regulated who could wear what.
Tuareg Metalwork
Tuareg · 18th c. onward · Sahara
Silver crosses, amulet cases, saddle fittings. Each Tuareg town has a signature cross shape; the Agadez cross is the most widely reproduced.
Ikenga
Igbo · pre-1900 · southeast Nigeria
Personal shrines carved as horned male figures, each one owned by a man and activated through sacrifice to embody the 'right hand' — the strength that accomplishes things. When the owner dies the Ikenga is split in two.
Sowei Helmet Mask
Mende · 19th c. onward · Sierra Leone
A black helmet mask worn in the Sande women's society initiation — the only African mask tradition danced exclusively by women, representing ideals of beauty and comportment.
Zulu Beadwork
Zulu · 19th c. onward · South Africa
Glass trade beads sewn onto leather and cotton in geometric patterns — colors and combinations encoding courtship messages ("love letters" in bead).
Chiwara Headdress
Bamana · 19th–20th century · Mali
A roan antelope carved in openwork wood worn by young men who danced in pairs at planting season. The Bamana say Chiwara is the mythical being who first taught humans to farm, bent over the earth like the antelope's graceful curve.
Kasubi Tombs
Buganda kingdom · 1882 · Uganda
The burial palace of four kabakas of Buganda, a vast thatched dome called Muzibu-Azaala-Mpanga supported by rings of wooden columns wrapped in bark cloth. Fire destroyed the main structure in 2010 and a painstaking reconstruction is ongoing.
Nkisi Power Figure
Kongo · 19th–20th c. · Central Africa
Wooden figures bristling with nails and blades. Each nail driven in activated a pledge, a curse, or a contract witnessed by the spirit inside.
Fang Reliquary (Byeri)
Fang · 19th–20th c. · Gabon / Cameroon
Carved guardian heads placed atop boxes of ancestral bones. Picasso saw one in 1907 and the Demoiselles d'Avignon followed.
Akuaba
Akan · traditional · Ghana
Wooden fertility figures carried by women hoping to conceive. Small, abstracted, round-faced. Carved also now for collectors; the sacred ones are never sold.
Herero Dress
Herero · late 19th c. onward · Namibia
Floor-length Victorian gowns adopted from 19th-century German missionaries — kept, inverted, made defiantly Herero. The horned headdress honors cattle; the silhouette accuses a colonial past.
Mbuti Bark Cloth
Mbuti · traditional · Ituri Rainforest
Beaten fig-bark cloth painted by women with dots, curves, and grids in charcoal, juice, and mud. Every panel is an individual composition — one of Africa's most abstract traditional visual idioms.
Ndebele House Painting
Ndebele · 20th century · South Africa
Exterior walls painted freehand by women in sharp-edged chevrons, diamonds, and vivid blocks of color, applied without grid or ruler. The practice emerged after the 1883 Ndebele defeat as a coded language of resistance preserved inside everyday domestic space.
Osun-Osogbo Sacred Grove
Yoruba · 20th-c. revival of traditional · Nigeria
A forest along the Oshun River filled with sculptures revived by the Austrian-Yoruba artist Susanne Wenger and local carvers — the last surviving intact sacred grove of the Yoruba.
Great Mosque of Touba
Mouride · 1963 · Senegal
Founded by the Sufi saint Amadou Bamba; the current mosque completed in the 1960s. Five minarets and a green dome; a gathering of millions every year at the Magal pilgrimage.
Shona Stone Sculpture
Shona · 20th c. onward · Zimbabwe
A post-colonial sculptural tradition carved from serpentine and springstone. Figures of spirits and ancestors, often semi-abstracted — now Zimbabwe's most recognized art export.
Red Bird
by Chris Ofili
Contemporary · c. 2000s · United Kingdom / Nigeria
Chris Ofili's paintings layer resin-coated elephant dung onto glittering collage. His work interrogates Black identity and the politics of representation in Britain.
←Americas Egypt & Nubia→
